league.name: La Liga
league.description: Spain’s top flight: technical football, strong youth academies, and two global giants in Real Madrid and Barcelona. Smaller clubs and regions (Basque Country, Seville, Valencia) add identity beyond the big two.
league.styleOfPlay: Traditionally more possession and touch in midfield; patience to find gaps. Defending is organised; attackers use dribbling and combinations. Real and Barça set the star standard, while others often defend deep and counter.
famousClubs: Real Madrid — European Cup record and galáctico eras
Barcelona — La Masia, tiki-taka, Catalan identity
Atlético Madrid — gritty defending and Diego Simeone style
Real Sociedad — Basque academy and possession football
famousPlayers: Vinícius Júnior — Real Madrid left winger
Jude Bellingham — Real Madrid midfielder and goal threat
Pedri — Barcelona midfielder, Spain youth graduate
league.historySummary: La Liga grew through the 20th century with Madrid and Barça at the centre. Spanish talent pools fed midfield artists and smart defenders. The league exported coaches and style worldwide, even as the big two dominate headlines and TV interest.
rivalries: El Clásico — Real Madrid vs Barcelona
Madrid derby — Real Madrid vs Atlético Madrid
Seville derby — Sevilla vs Real Betis
league.fanGuide: Start with El Clásico to see Spain’s biggest story. Notice how Barça fans tie club to Catalan culture; Madrid fans expect European nights. Late kickoff times (UK viewers) and strong regional derbies help you see beyond two clubs.

teams.name: Real Madrid
teams.shortHistory: Real Madrid measure success in European Cups — they have won the competition more than any other club. From Di Stéfano to the galácticos and modern stars, the story is star power, late goals, and pressure to win every season.
teams.fanGuide: White shirts at the Santiago Bernabéu; “Hala Madrid” is the chant to know. El Clásico vs Barcelona is the world’s most watched club fixture. The Madrid derby vs Atlético splits the capital. Expect talk of legends, transfers, and whether the team “knows how to win” in big moments.
legends: Alfredo Di Stéfano — forward who shaped the club’s first European dominance
Raúl — one-club striker; symbol of loyalty in white before the galácticos
Cristiano Ronaldo — record goalscorer in Madrid; four Champions Leagues there
Zinedine Zidane — playmaker turned manager; three straight European Cups as coach
currentKeyPlayers: Vinícius Júnior — left wing; dribbling and big-game goals
Jude Bellingham — midfielder; arrives in the box and leads by example
Federico Valverde — engine; covers ground in midfield or wide
Luka Modrić — veteran playmaker; outside-foot passes and control

teams.name: Barcelona
teams.shortHistory: Barcelona’s identity is La Masia — the academy that taught possession football to the world. The Messi era brought endless trophies; recent years mixed financial trouble with rebuilds built on Pedri, Gavi, and Catalan pride.
teams.fanGuide: Blaugrana stripes (blue and garnet); supporters call themselves culers. “Més que un club” means the team stands for Catalan identity as well as football. El Clásico vs Real Madrid is the must-learn fixture; the city derby vs Espanyol is smaller but fierce. “Cant del Barça” often plays after home wins.
legends: Lionel Messi — greatest player in club history; hundreds of goals in blaugrana
Xavi — midfield metronome; defined tiki-taka as player and coach
Andrés Iniesta — clutch creator; World Cup winner and fan favourite
Carles Puyol — captain; warrior centre-back of the 2000s–2010s
currentKeyPlayers: Robert Lewandowski — striker; movement and finishing in the box
Pedri — midfielder; press resistance and passing
Gavi — midfielder; energy, tackling, and bite
Raphinha — right wing; dribbling and direct runs

teams.name: Atlético Madrid
teams.shortHistory: Atlético built a reputation as the capital’s gritty alternative to Real’s glamour — Simeone’s era added titles through organisation, set pieces, and fan intensity.
teams.fanGuide: Red and white stripes; the Madrid derby splits the city. “El Cholo” chants reference long-running manager Diego Simeone.

Diego Godín — defensive rock of title years
currentKeyPlayers: Antoine Griezmann — forward and creative hub

teams.name: Sevilla
teams.shortHistory: Sevilla became Europe’s specialist in the Europa League, winning the competition repeatedly while the big two dominated La Liga headlines.
teams.fanGuide: White shirts with red accents; the Seville derby vs Betis is one of Spain’s fiercest local rivalries.
legends: Diego Maradona — brief but iconic spell
Iván Rakitić — midfield engine of trophy eras

teams.name: Athletic Club
teams.shortHistory: Athletic Club operate a unique policy of fielding only players from the Basque Country. Despite this restriction they have never been relegated from La Liga, hold a historic relationship with their region, and have won eight league titles.
teams.fanGuide: San Mamés — nicknamed the Cathedral — is one of Europe's iconic football cathedrals. The Basque-only policy and their unique identity make Athletic Club genuinely one-of-a-kind.

teams.name: Real Sociedad
teams.shortHistory: San Sebastián club who won back-to-back La Liga titles in 1981 and 1982 and are known for developing Basque talent alongside foreign stars. Their modern era saw them qualify regularly for European competition.
teams.fanGuide: The Basque derby against Athletic Club — txuri-urdin against the reds and whites — is the Basque Country's defining football match.

teams.name: Real Betis
teams.shortHistory: Seville's green-and-white half, Betis won their only league title in 1935 and have enjoyed periodic revivals with exciting attacking football. The Seville derby against FC Sevilla is one of Spain's most atmospheric local derbies.
teams.fanGuide: Béticos are known for their passion and willingness to applaud good football, even from opponents. The derby against Sevilla FC fills the whole city.

teams.name: Villarreal
teams.shortHistory: The Yellow Submarine from a small town in Valencia province have become a consistent La Liga presence and Europa League winners in 2021 under Unai Emery, defeating Manchester United on penalties in the final.
teams.fanGuide: Villarreal is a small-town club that over-achieves through smart management and a clear footballing identity. Their Europa League win is the high-water mark of a remarkable rise.

teams.name: Celta Vigo
teams.shortHistory: Galician club from the far northwest corner of Spain, Celta Vigo are known for technical, attacking football shaped by their history of producing creative players from the region.
teams.fanGuide: The Galician derby against Deportivo is the local rivalry. Celta's sky-blue and white shirts and their Balaídos ground are the symbols of football in Galicia.

teams.name: Valencia
teams.shortHistory: Valencia were the dominant force in Spanish football at the turn of the millennium, reaching two consecutive Champions League finals in 2000 and 2001 and winning La Liga twice. Mestalla is one of Spain's most intimidating atmospheres.
teams.fanGuide: Mestalla's steep, close stands create a cauldron effect that has rattled visiting Champions League contenders for decades. The club's financial troubles contrast with a proud history.
